coredata.dc:title: Methods for faster estimation of the entropy profile of a lithium-ion battery: A comparison of accelerated potentiometry and the estimation of entropy through thermal signatures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Jinsong
Vie, Preben J.S.
Wind, Julia
coredata.dc:description: The operating temperature of a lithium-ion battery (LIB) has strong effects on its electrochemical performance, safety, and lifetime. Reliable predictions of cell temperature and heat generation rate are required for the design and thermal management of battery systems. The entropy variation of a LIB has a significant influence on the heat generation and the cell temperature variation, especially at a lower C-rate. Since the entropy is a function of State-of-Charge (SoC), and may change during ageing, the entropy profile may as well be used as an indicator for battery State-of-Health diagnostics. The present experimental methods, such as potentiometric or calorimetric methods, for determining the entropy variation throughout a battery's full SoC window are time-consuming and require specialized equipment with good thermal insulation and instrumentation. Hence, faster and more convenient methods are needed. In this paper, we compare an accelerated potentiometric method with a new entropy characterization method where the entropy profile is extracted from the thermal signature of a LIB during charge and discharge at low C-rates. The accelerated potentiometric method aims to obtain the entropy from thermal cycling while the cell is still not completely relaxed to its stable OCV. The thermal signature method monitors the variations of a cell's outer surface temperature during a low constant current charge or discharge, from which the continuous variation of entropy with SoC can be extracted. The obtained entropy profile was validated through a modeling approach that combines a P2D-electrochemical model for battery operation and a 3D-finite-element model for the thermal behavior during battery operation. Good agreement between the model predictions and experiments was achieved on the temporal variation of the cell's outer surface temperature.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup for thermal signature measurement for entropy variation test with the calorimetric method. A) The cell with a temperature sensor (Pt-100) and cables for current and voltage is placed on a 30 mm-thick insulation foam sheet. B) The cell is covered with the same type 30 mm-thick foam sheet with 10 mm aluminum plates on top to act as thermal mass. C) Side view of thermal insulation setup with test cables. Additional pieces of 10 mm-thick foam are added in cracks to reduce thermal leakage. The whole setup is put inside a temperature-controlled chamber.

Schematic of the numerical modeling approach: (a) P2D electrochemical battery model to simulate the electrochemical performance and local heat generation, and (b) 3D finite-element thermal model for prediction of the thermal balance and cell operating temperature.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the cells used in this paper and their initial capacity.

Results from the entropy measurement at 47 % SoC during discharge for cell_03 shown in Fig. 5. The results from each temperature step are presented. The entropy from this step is then calculated as the average of the four results being 129 ± 7 µV/K.

head.ce:title: Methods for faster estimation of the entropy profile of a lithium-ion battery: A comparison of accelerated potentiometry and the estimation of entropy through thermal signatures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Energy Technology, 2007 Kjeller, Norway
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Energy Technology, 2007 Kjeller, Norway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The operating temperature of a lithium-ion battery (LIB) has strong effects on its electrochemical performance, safety, and lifetime. Reliable predictions of cell temperature and heat generation rate are required for the design and thermal management of battery systems. The entropy variation of a LIB has a significant influence on the heat generation and the cell temperature variation, especially at a lower C-rate. Since the entropy is a function of State-of-Charge (SoC), and may change during ageing, the entropy profile may as well be used as an indicator for battery State-of-Health diagnostics. The present experimental methods, such as potentiometric or calorimetric methods, for determining the entropy variation throughout a battery's full SoC window are time-consuming and require specialized equipment with good thermal insulation and instrumentation. Hence, faster and more convenient methods are needed. In this paper, we compare an accelerated potentiometric method with a new entropy characterization method where the entropy profile is extracted from the thermal signature of a LIB during charge and discharge at low C-rates. The accelerated potentiometric method aims to obtain the entropy from thermal cycling while the cell is still not completely relaxed to its stable OCV. The thermal signature method monitors the variations of a cell's outer surface temperature during a low constant current charge or discharge, from which the continuous variation of entropy with SoC can be extracted. The obtained entropy profile was validated through a modeling approach that combines a P2D-electrochemical model for battery operation and a 3D-finite-element model for the thermal behavior during battery operation. Good agreement between the model predictions and experiments was achieved on the temporal variation of the cell's outer surface temperature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the steady performance improvements in terms of energy and power densities, Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are extensively used in various applications such as energy storage systems for renewable energy sources, battery and hybrid electric ships, electric vehicles, and portable electronics [1]. The technological challenges are to increase their energy densities while maximizing their performance, prolong their lifetime, and ensure safety and reliability during operation. Operating temperature is one of the major parameters affecting battery performance such as power output, capacity, safety, and cycle life [2,3]. The cell temperature is affected by not only its operating environment, including cooling or heating but also the heat generation/consumption during charge and discharge. To maintain an optimal operating temperature range for a battery system, a cost-effective and efficient battery thermal management system is required [4]. Therefore, it is essential to understand the thermodynamic relationship between heat generation/consumption and battery operation to accurately predict the temperature variation of the cell during operation [5].

The heat sources in a LIB are normally classified into irreversible heat (
Q
i r r
) due to ohmic resistance, charge-transfer overpotentials, and mass-transfer limitations; and reversible heat (
Q
r e v
) from entropy change
Δ
S
[4,6–8]. The irreversible heat generation is related to the cell's internal resistance (
R i
) and the applied current ( I ) as
Q ˙
i r r
∝
R i
I
2
. The reversible heat depends on the entropy variation (
Δ
S
) of electrode materials as function of state of charge (
S o C
), and temperature ( T ). They are correlated as
Q ˙
r e v
∝ I
T
Δ
S
(
S o C
)
. The entropy variation (∆S) is related to structural changes of the active materials during charge or discharge. When the current is low, the reversible heat source is normally larger than the irreversible heat source [9]. As the current increases, the irreversible heat source will eventually become the dominating heat source. Therefore, the entropy variation of a LIB can be measured more accurately at low C-rates. 1
1 The C-rate is the measure of the rate at which a battery is being charged or discharged relative to its maximum capacity. A 1C rate means that the discharge current will discharge the entire battery in 1 hour. An accurate measurement of entropy-variation will not only help to improve the battery operation under optimal thermal conditions but can also be used to diagnose battery aging [10,11] since it can reveal changes in cell balancing as well as changes in the electrode structure [12,13].

Available techniques for measuring the entropy variation of a battery can mainly be classified into potentiometric [14,15] and calorimetric methods [9,16–19]. The potentiometric method measures the change in open circuit voltage (
U
o c v
) as a function of a change in cell temperature ( T ). The entropy variation with SoC is obtained by
Δ
S
(

)
= n · F ·

∂ T
, where n is the stoichiometric number of electrons participating in the cell reaction (
n = 1
for a LIB) and F is the Faraday constant (
F = 96 , 485
C / m o l
). This method normally requires a long relaxation time to reach the equilibrium state for the open circuit voltage (OCV) following each change in SoC and temperature [15]. Consequently, it may take several days or weeks to get the entropy variation over the entire SoC range. The calorimetric method is based on measuring the heat generation during charge and discharge, and various models [9,16–19] are applied to differentiate between and quantify the reversible (
Q
r e v
) and irreversible (
Q
i r r
) heat sources. The entropy variation with SoC is obtained via the reversible heat rate (
Q ˙
r e v

Δ

Q ˙

· T
. In classical calorimetry [17], a calorimeter is used to measure the generated heat during charge or discharge. The calorimetric measurements are analyzed to determine the proportion of reversible heat in the overall heat generated. The calorimeter equipment can be quite expensive. In summary, the potentiometric method is considered an accurate method to measure the battery entropy variation and it is commonly used as a reference for the verification of the results obtained by other methods. However, it is very time-consuming and requires a temperature-controlled environment. To speed up the measurement of the classic potentiometric method, Osswald et al. [13] proposed an OCV relaxation background subtraction method. They demonstrated an accurate measurement of the temperature response of OCV even if the potential is still relaxing towards the equilibrium. The time-dependent background drift can be determined and subtracted from the raw data before extracting
∂
U
o c v
∂ T
. Hu et al. [20] proposed an improved potentiometric method based on a positive adjustment (PA), which is an effective approach to adjusting the voltage relaxation curve. However, for these techniques, the battery cell still needs to relax for a considerable time before conducting the entropy measurement. Although the classical calorimetric method needs expensive calorimeter equipment to measure the heat generation, more recently developed calorimetric methods try to estimate the battery's heat generation and thermal balance through measuring the cell surface heat flux and monitoring the cell temperature, which is relatively inexpensive and fast.

Schmidt et al. [7] proposed to extract the reversible heat based on electrothermal impedance spectroscopy (ETIS) that can be up to 100 times faster than the potentiometric method: a high-frequency current superimposed on a DC offset current is applied while monitoring the battery surface temperature. The entropy profile is then obtained by Fourier analysis of the battery temperature to estimate the reversible heat generation since reversible and irreversible heat sources have different responding characteristics to the applied current (
Q
r e v
∝ I
and
Q
i r r
∝
I
2
). Murashko et al. [18] proposed a method to determine the entropy profile by heat flux measurements on the surface of a cylindrical LIB. The advantages of this method are that it does not require an expensive calorimeter for the measurements of heat generation, and the time for the test is reduced to only 80 min per SoC step. Damay et al. [19] proposed a fast calorimetric method to obtain a continuous entropy profile. The cell surface temperature is monitored while the battery is charged and discharged continuously. A thermal balance model is used to estimate the heat generation from the changes in the monitored cell temperature during charge and discharge. The reversible proportion of the total heat generation is estimated with the assumption that the irreversible heat generations during charge and discharge cycles are identical. The benefits of this method are low cost, fast, and high resolution.

Recently, more calorimetric methods have been proposed to measure the entropy variation in a more time- and cost-effective manner. Geng et al. [9] proposed a method to determine the entropic coefficient for a large commercial lithium-ion cell, even for cells in situ in a pack. In this method, an alternating square current is applied within a small SoC range to create a temperature swing, and the entropic coefficient can be obtained by Fourier analysis. Bedürftig et al. [8] proposed a calorimetric method named ‘Double Pulse Method’ to measure reversible heat in LIB cells by exciting the cell with charge and discharge current pulses. The reversible heat is determined by subtracting the resulting temperature increase of two current pulses of opposite polarity. The advantages of this method include a simple evaluation of the measured signals, the use of cost-effective equipment, and a simple measurement setup. Ahmad et al. [21] proposed a fast entropy extraction method, which determines the entropic heat by analyzing the electro-thermal response of a battery to a sequence of constant current pulses, i.e., the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT). The general energy balance principle for batteries is utilized to characterize the generated heat into polarization heat and entropic heat corresponding to each applied intermittent galvanostatic pulse from the interleaved thermal signal.

In this paper, two entropy measurement techniques are presented and compared. First, an accelerated potentiometric method is applied in which the OCV relaxation is allowed to relax “long enough”, but not completely so that the effects of entropy are still measurable through regression and extrapolation. Subsequently, a new calorimetric method is proposed for an even faster estimation of the entropy profile of a LIB via monitoring the battery temperature variations during three constant current charge and discharge tests. To enhance the reversible heat (or cooling) and reduce the irreversible heat, low current rates (e.g. C/20, C/10, and C/5) are suggested. To magnify the effects of entropic heating, the heat loss should be minimized and the battery is then thermally insulated. By studying the thermal balance and the variation of battery temperature with SoC during charge and discharge, the reversible heat generation rate as a function of SoC can be extracted and the entropy variation is hence derived. The entropy profile obtained by this method is verified through a direct comparison with the results from the accelerated potentiometric method. In addition, the entropy profile obtained through the proposed calorimetric method is used as a key input parameter to an electrochemical battery model in COMSOL to simulate the battery temperature variation during charge or discharge [22,23]. The battery temperature variations predicted by the simulations are compared with those obtained in the experiments to validate the numerical model.

This paper is structured as follows: Section 2 gives the fundamental background of the potentiometric and calorimetric methods, the general thermal balance of a battery during charge and discharge, and the derivation of the proposed method to extract the entropy variation. The assumptions made in the theoretical derivation and the design of the experimental setup are also discussed. Section 3 describes the experimental setup for the accelerated potentiometric method and the experimental procedure to measure the temperature profiles that are used in the proposed calorimetric method for entropy profile extraction. Section 4 presents the methodology for modeling the battery's temperature variations during charge or discharge to verify the entropy profile obtained in the experiments. Section 5 provides the results from the accelerated potentiometric method, the temperature profile obtained in experiments, and its derived entropy profile using the methods presented in Section 2.3. The calculated entropy profile from the temperature profiles is compared with those from the potentiometric method. The deviations are discussed and compared with results reported in the literature. Numerical model predictions of the battery's temperature variations during charge and discharge are compared with the measured temperature variations from the characterization tests. Section 6 concludes with a summary of the findings, and the potential for future research and application are discussed.

In the potentiometric method [14,15], the battery cell is put into a temperature-controlled chamber with a defined stable temperature. Then, the cell is charged or discharged to a given SoC, followed by a period of relaxation to reach its “pseudo” equilibrium open circuit voltage (
U
o c v
). Finally, a thermal cycle is imposed on the cell by changing the temperature ( T ) of the temperature-controlled chamber to different values and measuring the corresponding change in the
U
o c v
at the different stable temperatures of the cell. The entropy coefficient (
∂
U
o c v
∂ T
) is then obtained as the linearly fitted slope of
U
o c v
and T . Due to fact that OCV theoretically never reach a stable asymptotic value, another typical approach is to calculate the entropy coefficient based on each temperature step (
Δ
U
o c v
Δ
T
). The measurement process described above is repeated usually for 10 to 50 discrete points of SoC that cover the whole battery SoC range, and a lookup table of the entropy coefficient as a function of SoC is created. This method is accurate, reliable, and non-destructive, and has been widely applied to various types of battery cells.

To achieve an accurate result with the potentiometric method, high-precision voltage measurements are required, and the cell must reach its equilibrium state at each measurement point. Due to the long time required to reach both thermal equilibrium and OCV equilibrium, the potentiometric method is extremely time-consuming. Depending on a battery's size, geometry, and chemistry, it can take several hours to several days to reach one pseudo-OCV equilibrium value. For example, if 6 h are necessary to reach a stable pseudo-OCV and 4 h are needed to obtain thermal equilibrium at several temperatures, an entropy profile with 20 SoC datapoints for both charge and discharge would require about 3 weeks of measurement time.

In this paper, we apply an accelerated variant of the potentiometric method where the temperature cycle is allowed to start earlier, i.e., while the cell still has a relaxation rate of >1 mV/h. To correct for the change in OCV due to the relaxation and enhance the entropy effect, the OCV relaxation curve is extrapolated based on data preceding the temperature change: Upon reaching a new stable temperature (and voltage), the voltage is extrapolated back to the center point between the end of the first stable temperature period and the beginning of the next stable temperature period. The difference between the two extrapolated regression curves then represents the OCV change due to the change in cell temperature and the entropy at the specified SoC. A more detailed description of this method is provided in the experimental section below.

The general energy balance for battery systems was presented by e.g. Bernardi et al. [4]. The heat evolution inside a battery changes during its operation and can be categorized as (1) polarization heat (
Q ˙
p o l
), (2) entropic heat (
Q ˙
e n t
), (3) heat due to phase changes (
Q ˙
p c h
), (4) heat of mixing (
Q ˙
m i x
), and (5) heat generated by side reactions (
Q ˙
r e a
). The total heat generation rate
Q ˙
t o t
(W) can be expressed as
(1)

The heat generated by side reactions (

) is responsible for parasitic aging reactions inside the battery and is normally an insignificant heat as discussed by Forgez et al. [24]. The heat of mixing (

) corresponds to the generation and relaxation of ionic concentration profiles in the battery and is neglectable at low C-rates [25]. The heat due to phase changes (

p c h
) can also be ignored if no phase changes other than crystalline phase transitions occur for all species involved in the electrochemical reaction, which is the case in LIBs without side reactions [25]. Hence, the main contribution to the total heat generation in a lithium-ion battery is the heat due to polarization (

The polarization heat is the result of the applied current and several overpotentials, which are predominantly due to the ohmic losses in the ionic and various electronic resistances, charge transfer resistance at the electrode-electrolyte interface, and mass transport (diffusion) limitations. The polarization heat is positive during both charge and discharge thus always producing heating to the battery. It is therefore considered an irreversible heat source (
Q ˙
i r r
).

The entropic heat corresponds to structural changes during the intercalation and deintercalation processes inside the active materials of the battery [6]. It shows the reversible feature during the battery charge and discharge operations. The reversible heat (

To measure the entropy through temperature variations and achieve high accuracy, the entropy should be the dominating heat source, and the irreversible heat should be minimized. Hence, low C-rates should be applied. Additionally, the heat loss should be minimized, so that the measured temperature caused by the entropy variation is enhanced. Therefore, the cell should be thermally insulated, and the ambient temperature be kept constant.

The heat loss coefficient h in Eqs. (5)–(9) is not measured directly during the characterization test. It can, however, be estimated through an optimization process so that the entropy coefficients obtained under different C-rates have a minimal difference.

The battery used in this study was a large-format commercial pouch cell (XALT 31HE) with the following key specifications:
•
Active materials: NMC111 for cathode and graphite for anode,
•
Nominal capacity: 31 Ah,
•
End-of-charge voltage: 4.2 V,
•
End-of-discharge voltage: 2.7 V,
•
Outer cell dimension: 226 mm X 224 mm X 7.2 mm.

The detailed layer thicknesses of the anode electrode, cathode electrode, separator, and current collectors were measured after disassembly of a battery cell. The cell layer structure, battery materials, and thermal properties are summarized in Table 1 [26,27]. For more details on this cell we refer to our earlier work, including results from a cycle-life study and incremental capacity analysis [28] as well as thermal conductivities [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Entropy measurement using a potentiometric method
ce:section.ce:para:
Following the principle of the potentiometric method, the entropy at any specified SoC was measured by recording the change in OCV while changing the temperature. The measurement system was built based on a National Instruments PXI-1045 chassis 2
2
https://www.ni.com/en-no/shop/pxi.html
and a PXI-8115 controller, using high-precision modules for high-accuracy measurements of voltage, current, and temperature (PXI-4071, PXI-4070), while assuring high-accuracy control of the voltage and current through four source measure units (NI-4130). Additionally, a multiplexer (PXI-2501), relay switch (PXI-2565), and RS232 serial interface (PXI-8430/4) were used to enable control and logging of four cells simultaneously as well as controlling a high-precision water bath controller (Julabo SE-Z) with an accuracy of 0.01 °C. The cells were immersed in the water bath during the entropy measurements. Before the immersion, the cells were placed into thick-walled plastic bags (e.g. plastic bags used for vacuuming food) with a temperature sensors (Pt-100) attached to the center of the cell surface. This separated the cells from the water avoiding local shorts of the cells through the water, while still ensuring good thermal contact and hence a stable temperature. The potentiometric entropy measurement was performed by the following protocol when a stable temperature was obtained in the water bath:
1. Charge cell to 4.2 V (100 % SoC) (CCCV charge with 0.05C current cut-off).
2. Allow cell voltage (OCV) to stabilize to a relaxation rate lower than 1.4 mV/h or a maximum relaxation time of 6 h.
3. Cycle the temperature of the water bath from 25 °C to 16 °C, and back to 25 °C via steps of 3 °C increments from 16 °C. At every temperature step, the OCV and temperature were allowed to stabilize before the next temperature step. The temperature was assumed stable when the standard deviation of a five-minute time window was smaller than 0.01 °C. The OCV of the cell was recorded continuously.
4. The SoC was changed in steps of approximately 5 % through a constant 0.05C current (CC) discharge or charge. (The cell will in steps of 5 % SoC eventually reach 2.7 V (0 % SoC) and then be charged back to 100 % SoC in 5 % SoC steps)
5. Loop back to step 2.

The entropy value for each SoC was calculated from the voltage and temperature measurements as follows: An exponential regression curve was fitted to the OCV relaxation following the current pulse to reach this SoC value (step 2 above) based on the last maximum 30 min before the temperature is changed to the first temperature step (step 3). A linear regression curve is used to fit the voltage response following each of the four temperature changes (step 3) when the cell temperature again was stable for a maximum of 20 min. The regression curves were extrapolated to the center between the end of the first stable temperature period and the beginning of the next stable temperature period. The entropy voltage for each temperature step was then calculated from the difference between the extrapolated voltage regression curves at the center value. The entropy value at a given SoC was calculated as the average of the four calculated entropy values from the four temperature steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal signature measurement for the proposed calorimetric method
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental setup for thermal signature measurement for the entropy profile test with the proposed calorimetric method is shown in Fig. 1 , details on the used materials, including their thermal properties, are summarized in Table 2 . The cell was surrounded by insulating material to minimize heat loss and secure a homogeneous cell temperature. All outer surfaces of the test cell were covered with polyurethane foam plates with a thickness of 30 mm. On top of the insulating layers, an aluminum plate with a thickness of 10 mm is placed to act as a thermal mass to stabilize the ambient temperature. One temperature sensor was mounted at the center of the upper surface of the cell. The ambient temperature sensor was attached to the outer surface of the top aluminum plate. The used temperature sensors (Pt-100) have a high-temperature sensitivity. The whole setup was placed in a temperature chamber, with a constant inner temperature of 298 ± 0.1 K.

To maximize the ratio between the reversible heat and the irreversible heat, the applied current needs to be as low while still being able to produce a significant temperature signature to offer a good signal-to-noise ratio. Three currents (C/5, C/10, and C/20) were chosen in this paper. The following characterization test protocol was used:
•
CCCV charge to 4.2 V (0.05C cut-off current; 100 % SoC),
•
Rest to thermal equilibrium,
•
CC discharge to 2.7 V,
•
Rest to thermal equilibrium,
•
CCCV charge to 4.2 V (0.05C cut-off current),
•
Rest to thermal equilibrium.
During the tests, both cell voltage and temperature were recorded for the subsequent extraction of the entropy profile.

To verify the results of the proposed calorimetric method, a modeling approach coupling an electrochemical model (P2D model, see below) with a 3D finite-element thermal model (as implemented in the commercial COMSOL battery design module) was employed. The P2D sub-model [22] is used to simulate the electrochemical behavior and estimate the local volumetric heat source. This is coupled to a 3D FEM sub-model to simulate heat conduction within a simplified battery geometry including the thermal insulation layer and aluminum plates (cf. experimental setup in Fig. 1). A schematic of this modeling approach is illustrated in Fig. 2 . The entropy coefficient obtained by the calorimetric method is included in the P2D model to predict the volumetric heat generation. The simulated cell temperature variations are directly compared to those obtained in the entropy measurements.

A pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) electrochemical battery model as implemented in the COMSOL design module is set up to simulate the fundamental electrochemical processes within the battery. The following equations for the conservation of mass and charge are solved.

During the charging process, lithium ions de-intercalate from the cathode active material (e.g., NMC) and insert the anode active material (graphite) through the electrolyte. It reverses during the discharging process. The diffusion of lithium ions within the electrode particles is described by Fick's second law,

The parameter set used for P2D modeling is based on a combination of parameters extracted from our own cell-teardown analysis, supplemented by standard parameters for NMC and graphite available in literature as well as estimations followed by optimizations. The key parameters for the P2D model are summarized in Table 3 . The measured and balanced OCV curves for both electrodes are shown in Fig. 3 . Those were obtained from half-cell measurements on coin cells, followed by balancing the stoichiometric ranges against full cell potentials [30].

The thermal behavior of the battery during the characterization test is predicted by a 3D-finite-element (3D-FEM) model which includes the geometry-simplified battery, the thermal insulation layer, and the aluminum plates. The energy conservation for the battery characterization test setup system can be described by the heat conduction equation:
(25)

Since low C-rates are applied and the battery is well insulated, the thermal behavior within the battery can be assumed to be symmetric in all three spatial dimensions. Hence, only one-eighth part of the battery's volume needs to be simulated.

) is used by the P2D model to calculate the electrochemical kinetics, electrical behavior, and heat generation.

Three (out of a total of 56) XALT 31HE cells were used in this paper for the measurement of cell entropy profiles at 100 % SoH. Their initial capacities at 0.1C are summarized in Table 4 as well as their planned use after the entropy tests. All cells were used for measuring the entropy through the potentiometric method, while one cell (cell_03) was used to measure the entropy by measuring temperature signatures. The other two cells (cell_01 and cell_02) had already been aged as part of a cyclic ageing study [28] and were not available for the temperature signature method performed later.

The average capacity of a sample of 56 tested XALT cells was 33.45 ± 0.25 Ah. The three cells used in this study were all within the 95 % confidence interval, but outside the normal standard deviation: two cells have higher capacities, and one cell has lower. These differences can be observed in Fig. 4 . In addition to the differences in capacity, a slightly shifted electrode balance was observed for cell_02 compared to most other tested cells, evidenced in the incremental capacity (dQ/dV) plots.

The method for measuring the entropy through the accelerated potentiometric method for one selected SoC step (SoC = 47 %) is illustrated in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6
: The entropy measurement starts after a current pulse reaches the target SoC and once the voltage relaxation has reached a specified slope. The temperature is then looped through four consecutive temperature steps and the changes in cell voltages due to entropy are recorded accordingly. The cell voltage is fitted to an exponential regression curve for the first OCV relaxation after the current pulse, and linear regression curves for the four following stable temperature periods. The data points used for each regression curve are shown as the symbols in Fig. 5. The regression curves are further extrapolated to either side of the stable temperature periods as shown in Fig. 6. The entropy for each temperature step can then be calculated from the voltage step of the extrapolated voltage curves at the center of each temperature step. The calculated entropies for each temperature step are summarized in Table 5 .

The entropy for the three cells during discharge was measured by systematically changing the SoC in steps of approximately 5 % from fully charged to fully discharged. In addition, the entropy profile during charge was measured for cell_03. The obtained entropy curves for the three different cells are presented in Fig. 7 . We observe that all three cells have very similar entropy during discharge while there is a hysteresis for the measured entropy during charge. The observed hysteresis between discharge and charge entropy may be explained by phase transitions occurring at different SoCs during charge or discharge. The measurement error for each measurement point is at maximum 1.2 JK-1mol-1. This corresponds to a maximum measurement error of 2 % of the absolute measured entropy value.

Fig. 8 shows the variation of cell temperature with SoC during charge and discharge at different C-rates. Since the temperature inside the climate chamber remains constant
T
a m b
= 298
K
, the cell temperature difference (
T
d i f
=
T
c e l l
−
T
a m b
) is used to study the battery's heating and cooling during charge and discharge. In general, a higher current rate results in a larger cell temperature variation during both, charge and discharge. When the battery is charged within a SoC range from 0 to 10 %, the battery cell is cooled due to the entropic effect (negative entropy coefficient). Charging within a SoC range of 30 to 60 %, the battery heats up significantly due to the entropy changes (positive entropy coefficient), followed by cooling (negative coefficient) during charging within a SoC range of 70 to 100 %. During discharge, the effect of entropy changes is reversed: the battery temperature increases significantly in the SoC ranges of 100 to 70 % and 10 to 0 % and decreases between 60 and 30 % SoC. Due to the effect of entropy variation, the battery temperature change becomes more prominent during discharge. The cell temperature variation range during discharge at 0.2C is about 4.1 K, while the cell temperature variation during charge at 0.2C is about 3 K.

To extract the entropy coefficient variation (
∂
U

∂ T
) from the monitored cell temperature changes with SoC during charge and discharge following Eq. (9), both the derivative of cell temperature difference with respect to SoC (

) and the heat loss coefficient ( h ) are needed. Based on the variations of cell temperature difference with SoC shown in Fig. 8, the derivatives

for both charge and discharge can be obtained. To reduce the noise level in the original data, a digital processing technique is applied to remove the high-frequency noise and smoothen data. The heat loss coefficient from the battery to the ambient, cannot be accurately extracted based on the simple experimental setup described in Section 3.2. However, the possible range of the heat transfer coefficient can be estimated: Under perfect insulation conditions, the lowest possible heat loss can be assumed to be zero (
h = 0

). On the other hand, if the battery is not insulated and tested under good natural ventilation, the highest possible heat transfer coefficient around the battery can be assumed to be

. The estimated entropy coefficients under different current rates deviate from each other, having a relatively large deviation error from the average value as indicated by the dashed lines in Fig. 9. Theoretically, the entropy coefficient should have no dependency on the applied C-rate. Hence, the estimated entropy coefficients under different C-rates should have a minimum derivation error from each other. This can be achieved by optimizing the heat transfer coefficient within the estimated range of
0 < h < 5
W

. Fig. 10 shows the estimated entropy coefficient under different C-rates using the optimized heat transfer coefficient

. The resulting entropy coefficients have the smallest deviation error, and the effective entropy coefficient of the battery is then obtained by averaging the estimated values across the different C-rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the entropy profile by two methods
ce:section.ce:para: The measured entropy profiles for the two presented methods are compared in Fig. 11 for cell_03. We observe a general reasonable agreement. However, a deviation is observed especially within the SoC range from 5 to 30 %. A similar trend has been reported in literature [7–9,19] (cf. Fig. 8 in [7], Fig. 6 in [9], Fig. 11 in [9], and Fig. 6 in [19]) as well, where different calorimetric methods were used to measure the battery entropy coefficient and the classic potentiometric method was applied. Even though the batteries used in their studies have different cathode chemistries, the changes in entropy coefficients with SoC have a similar characteristic profile. The potentiometric method gives relatively smooth entropic coefficient within the SoC range from 5 to 30 %. The entropy coefficient by the calorimetric method has a higher peak when SoC is about 10 % and a lower valley when SoC is about 23 %. Schmidt et al. [7] also reported greater differences arise at low SoCs. They thought the deviations could be explained by the extremely long open circuit voltage relaxation, which is due to sluggish diffusion kinetics. Since the entropy for the potentiometric method is determined from the potential difference during temperature variation, a superimposed voltage relaxation influences the accuracy of the determined entropy.

As presented in Section 2 for the methodology of thermodynamics, the entropy coefficient can be measured by OCV changes with temperature or the reversible heat release during charge and discharge. In practice, it is challenging for the potentiometric method to measure OCV at the state of both electrically and thermally equilibrium, especially of large commercial battery cells. In addition, the OCV relaxation rate may change significantly with SoC. The reversible heat release during charge and discharge cannot be measured directly. It is always extracted from the total heat release based on assumptions (e.g., ignoring the hysteresis effects during charge and discharge) and thermal balance models. Further investigation is needed to understand the deviations of the entropy profiles obtained by the potentiometric and calorimetric methods.

According to the themodynamics of a battery during operation, the full-cell entropy profiles can be attributed to the contributions from each anode and cathode in the battery. Hence, good measurement of the entropy profile of the individual electrodes will help understanding the full-cell entropy profiles. Most of the current measurements of entropy profiles for graphite are based on a “slow” potentiometric method. We hope that the individual entropy profiles for anode and cathode can also be measured with the “fast” method in the future. Mercer et al. [31] reported that the enthalpy of graphite lithiation has a pronounced “valley” at a lithium fraction of ∼0.1 in graphite cf. Fig. 3(a) in [31]. This enthalpy vally may have an effect on the heat generation and the thermal signature during the full-cell operation which was not accounted for in our thermal signature method. An understanding of the thermal balance from both anode and cathode electrodes will help us to explain the deviations of the entropy profiles obtained by the potentiometric and calorimetric methods.

The P2D model accounts for the transfer of lithium ions between the electrodes due to intercalation and de-intercalation. Fig. 13 compares the variations of cell voltage with SoC during (a) charge and (b) discharge predicted by the P2D model and measured in the characterization tests. The variation of the stoichiometric ratio in the electrodes results in changes in the crystal structure and entropy. To verify the importance of entropy variation in predicting the thermal behavior of a LIB during charge and discharge [32], the full-cell entropy coefficient obtained from the calorimetric method (Section 5.3) is used to estimate the entropy coefficient of the cathode [11,33] (input parameter to the P2D model). Since the tested cell has a graphite anode, and the entropy coefficient of graphite is reasonably well established, the entropy profile from literature is used [33], see Fig. 12 (a). As the full-cell entropy change is attributed to the contributions of both electrodes [11,33], the entropy coefficient for the cathode is estimated by taking the difference between the measured full-cell entropy and the graphite entropy. The obtained cathode entropy profiles according to the full-cell entropy obtained by the calorimetric method and the potentiometric method are shown in Fig. 12(b). Significant deviations are found when the lithiation stoichiometry is high in the range from 0.6 to 0.8.

In addition, the average temperature obtained in the 3D FEM model (Eq. (27) is used to specify the operating temperature of the P2D model. The total heat generation rate (volumetric heat source) is calculated by the P2D model according to Eq. (24). The battery electrical performance predicted by the P2D model is illustrated in Fig. 13 by showing the variation of the battery terminal voltage during (a) charge and (b) discharge under different current rates. The variations of battery terminal voltage during charge and discharge under different current rates measured in the characterization tests are also shown in Fig. 8. They are in good agreement.

The total volumetric heat generation rate during charge and discharge predicted by the P2D model is input as the source term of the 3D-FEM model for the battery characterization test. Another important parameter for the 3D thermal model is the heat transfer coefficient around the battery test pack. Inside the climate chamber, the gas flow is controlled so that the temperature is maintained constant and uniform. The gas flow speed is varied depending on the heat load of the stored battery. In 3D thermal modeling, the convection heat transfer coefficient is tested by trial and error so that simulation results can meet the experimental observation best. The optimal heat transfer coefficient is set to
h = 10
W
K
− 1
m
− 2
, which is reasonable inside a climate chamber. Furthermore, the pouch-type battery in the 3D thermal is simplified as a 3D box, ignoring the geometric details of the layer structures. Instead, the average thermal properties, (e.g., density, specific heat, and thermal conductivity), are set for the 3D battery domain. A layer-thickness-weighted method is applied here to get the average thermal properties:

Fig. 14 shows a snapshot of the predicted temperature distribution within the battery characterization test pack when the battery is charged at
I
c _ r a t e
= 0.2 C
and
SoC = 60
%
. The battery has the highest temperature of about 300.65 K. The temperature distribution within the battery is quite homogenous. The outer surface of the battery test pack has the same temperature as the set value of the climate chamber 298 K. The temperature gradient occurs within the insulation layer near the battery due to its low thermal conductivity. The variations of temperature at the monitoring point C, located at the outer center of the battery, are recorded during the simulations so that they can be compared with those measured in experiments.

Fig. 15 shows the variation of cell temperature difference during (a) charge and (b) discharge at different current rates of C/20, C/10, and C/5 by the simulations based on the entropy profile obtained using the proposed calorimetric method. They are also compared with those measured in the battery characterization tests and they are in good agreement. It can be concluded that the proposed numerical modeling method captures the thermal characteristic profiles of the battery during charge and discharge. Under the low current rate, the entropy variation contributes more significantly to the battery's thermal behavior. Hence, the entropy variation estimated using the proposed calorimetric method is reasonably accurate, which is verified by the numerical simulation of the thermal behavior of a LIB during charge and discharge.

Fig. 16 shows the variation of cell temperature difference during (a) charge and (b) discharge at different current rates of C/20, C/10, and C/5 by the simulations based on the entropy profile obtained using the potentiometric method and those measured by the characterization tests. Even though the overall trends of temperature differences predicted by simulations agree with those by the measurements, significant deviations are observed in the beginning period of charge and the ending period of discharge. Hence, the entropy profile obtained using the calorimetric method can reflect better the detailed thermal signature of a battery during charge and discharge.

An accelerated potentiometric and a new calorimetric method were developed for faster estimation of the entropy profiles of a lithium battery. The accelerated potentiometric method aims to obtain the entropy from thermal cycling while the cell is still not completely relaxed to its stable OCV. The new calorimetric method is proposed for fast determination of the entropy variation profile while the battery is charged and discharged under constant current. The variation of cell temperature with SoC is monitored. To enhance the contribution of entropic heat proportion to the heating/cooling of the battery, low current rates of C/20, C/10, and C/5 are applied to charge and discharge the battery. To ensure the battery temperature variation is measureable, the battery is insulated to minimize the heat loss to the ambient. By studying the battery temperature variation with SoC while charging and discharging and analyzing the thermal balance (with heat loss correction), the entropic coefficient with SoC can be derived. In summary, the proposed calorimetric method can provide a fast estimation of the entropy variation with continuous changes in SoC. The equipment for the characterization test is quite simple with a thermal insulation layer.

To verify the entropy variation obtained using the proposed new calorimetric method, it is compared with that obtained from the accelerated potentiometric method, and they are in reasonable agreement. In addition, the entropy variation is used as a key input parameter to an electrochemical battery model implemented in COMSOL to simulate the battery temperature variation during the charging or discharging characterization tests. The simulation predictions of the temporal variations of battery temperature agree well with those obtained in the measurements.
